original_abstract: The rapid rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria poses a significant challenge to conventional wound care. In this study, we developed an innovative, antibiotic-free antimicrobial strategy utilizing hybrid nanozymes designed for enhanced wound healing. These hybrid nanozymes (FMA NPs) were engineered by facilitating the in-situ growth of ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) onto metal-organic framework (MOF)-stabilized $Fe_3O_4$ nanoparticles. The resulting architecture exhibits synergistic peroxidase (POD)-like activity, generating high concentrations of lethal hydroxyl radicals (·OH) even at low, physiologically tolerable concentrations of $H_2O_2$ (0.97 mM). Our findings demonstrate that FMA nanozymes possess exceptional biocompatibility and exert potent antibacterial effects against both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) pathogens. In vivo experiments further confirmed that these nanozymes significantly accelerate wound repair while maintaining a favorable biosafety profile. This well-defined hybrid nanozyme platform represents a promising, non-toxic alternative for the treatment of bacterial infections, offering a robust tool for future antimicrobial therapies.
humanized_abstract: Antibiotic resistance is becoming a significant obstacle to modern wound care. Herein, we designed an antibiotic-free and sustainable antimicrobial strategy to advance wound healing using hybrid nanozymes. We developed an efficient method for the in-situ growth of ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) onto Metal-organic Framework (MOF) immobilized $Fe_3O_4$ nanoparticles and coined them as Fe-MOF-Au (FMA) NPs. The hybrids exhibit both POD-mimicking activity and produce large amounts of .OH at a low concentration of $H_2O_2$ (0.97 mM). Importantly, the FMA NPs showed good biocompatibility as well as excellent antibacterial effects against both Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) while enhancing wound healing and safety in vivo. In summary, this highly defined hybrid nanozymes technology holds great promise for the development of safe, efficient antibacterial drugs free from the risks of antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

Importantly, the FMA NPs showed good biocompatibility as well as excellent antibacterial effects against both Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) while enhancing wound healing and safety in vivo. In summary, this highly defined hybrid nanozymes technology holds great promise for the development of safe, efficient antibacterial drugs free from the risks of antibiotic-resistant bacteria.
